2023 · TMLR · Agents

Voyager: An Open-Ended Embodied Agent with Large Language Models

The first LLM-powered lifelong-learning agent in Minecraft: a frozen GPT-4 writes its own curriculum, grows an external library of reusable code skills, and debugs itself from environment feedback and self-verification — no gradients, no fine-tuning.

Guanzhi Wang, Yuqi Xie, Yunfan Jiang, Ajay Mandlekar, Chaowei Xiao, Yuke Zhu, Linxi "Jim" Fan, Anima Anandkumar · NVIDIA · Caltech · UT Austin · Stanford · UW-Madison
venue TMLR citations 1,831 topic Agents source ↗
// citation count as of June 2026
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

Building an agent that keeps getting better in an open-ended world — proposing its own goals, mastering skills, and reusing them later — is a long-standing grand challenge. Classical embodied agents learn from primitive actions via reinforcement or imitation learning: sample-hungry, hard to interpret, and brittle when asked to generalize. The newer wave of LLM agents (ReAct, Reflexion, AutoGPT) injected real world knowledge, but they are short-horizon planners: they reason-and-act inside a single episode and then forget. None are lifelong learners that accumulate, refine, and transfer skills across an extended lifetime.

Minecraft is the ideal crucible. It imposes no fixed goal and no storyline — just a procedurally generated 3D world and a tech tree (wood → stone → iron → diamond) that rewards compositional, long-horizon skill-building, exactly the way a human player ramps from mining wood to crafting diamond tools. The question Voyager poses: can a frozen LLM, queried only as a black box, drive its own curriculum, build a growing memory of skills, and keep discovering — with no human in the loop and no gradient updates?

// how it works

The method

Voyager wraps a frozen GPT-4 in three interacting loops, and makes one pivotal design choice: the action space is code, not low-level motor commands. Programs naturally express temporally extended, compositional behaviors — and, crucially, they can call one another, so skills compound. All control runs through the high-level Mineflayer JavaScript API; Voyager's focus is pushing GPT-4 as a planner, not solving 3D perception or sensorimotor control.

1 · Automatic curriculum

GPT-4 proposes the next task conditioned on the agent's live state — inventory, biome, health, hunger, and what it has already discovered — under the standing directive to "discover as many diverse things as possible." This is an in-context form of novelty search: difficulty scales to the agent's situation (harvest sand and cactus in a desert before chasing iron), so the agent is always handed a task of suitable complexity.

2 · Skill library

Every time a task succeeds, the generating program is committed to an external skill library. The key is the embedding of a GPT-3.5-written natural-language description of the program; the value is the program itself. To solve a new task, Voyager first has GPT-3.5 draft a general suggestion for the task, combines it with environment feedback as the query, embeds that, and retrieves the top-5 most relevant skills to drop into the prompt as in-context examples. New skills are written by composing old ones, so ability compounds — and because the memory lives outside the weights, the agent sidesteps catastrophic forgetting entirely.

retrieved = top-k s ∈ library  sim( e(query),  e(descs) ) // the query (a GPT-3.5 suggestion for the task ⊕ environment feedback) is embedded and the top-5 skills are retrieved from a vector DB by embedding similarity (k = 5)

3 · Iterative prompting mechanism

GPT-4's code is executed in the world, and three feedback signals are fed back for refinement: (a) environment feedback (e.g. "I cannot make an iron chestplate because I need 7 more iron ingots"); (b) execution errors (the JavaScript runtime trace); and (c) self-verification — a separate GPT-4 instance acts as a critic, inspecting the agent's state against the task and returning success/failure plus a critique. Voyager re-generates code until self-verification signs off (or a retry budget is spent), at which point the curriculum advances. Nothing here touches model weights — Voyager is pure prompting and in-context learning over a fixed GPT-4 snapshot.

// what they found

The results

With GPT-4, Voyager discovers 63 unique items in 160 prompting iterations — 3.3× more than its counterparts — and traverses 2.3× longer distances across the map. On the tech tree it unlocks the wooden tier 15.3× faster, the stone tier 8.5× faster, and the iron tier 6.4× faster than the best baseline, and is the only method to reach the diamond tool (102 iterations, in 1 of 3 runs).

The text-agent baselines — built for other domains and re-implemented here — collapse: ReAct and Reflexion fail to unlock any tier (0/3), and AutoGPT needs 92–135 iterations where Voyager needs 6–21 (Table 1). The skill library is also plug-and-play: bolted onto AutoGPT, it lets that agent solve held-out tasks it otherwise cannot.

Tech-tree mastery (Table 1)

Numbers are prompting iterations (fewer is better), averaged over three runs; N/A (0/3) means the method never unlocked that tier within 160 iterations.

MethodWoodenStoneIronDiamond
ReActN/A (0/3)N/A (0/3)N/A (0/3)N/A (0/3)
ReflexionN/A (0/3)N/A (0/3)N/A (0/3)N/A (0/3)
AutoGPT92 ± 7294 ± 72135 ± 103N/A (0/3)
Voyager w/o Skill Library7 ± 29 ± 429 ± 11N/A (0/3)
Voyager (Ours)6 ± 211 ± 221 ± 7102 (1/3)

Zero-shot generalization to a fresh world (Table 2)

Cleared inventory, brand-new world, unseen tasks, 50-iteration budget. Voyager solves all four; ReAct, Reflexion, and AutoGPT solve none.

MethodDiamond PickaxeGolden SwordLava BucketCompass
ReAct / Reflexion / AutoGPTN/A (0/3)N/A (0/3)N/A (0/3)N/A (0/3)
AutoGPT w/ Our Skill Library39 (1/3)30 (1/3)N/A (0/3)30 (2/3)
Voyager w/o Skill Library36 (2/3)30 ± 927 ± 926 ± 3
Voyager (Ours)19 ± 318 ± 721 ± 518 ± 2

Ablations are decisive

Replacing the automatic curriculum with a random one drops item discovery by 93%. Removing self-verification — the most important feedback type — costs −73%. And swapping GPT-4 for GPT-3.5 in code generation yields 5.7× fewer unique items, underscoring how much Voyager leans on GPT-4's coding ability specifically.

// does it hold up?

The eval read

The multipliers are eye-catching, but they rest on a thin and partly self-graded foundation. Three things to hold in mind.

First, n = 3, with variance that swallows the means. Every cell in Tables 1–2 averages three runs, and the spreads are enormous: AutoGPT unlocks wood in 92 ± 72 iterations and iron in 135 ± 103. The signature win — reaching the diamond tool — happened in one of three runs (102 iterations); the other two failed outright. The much-quoted "15.3× faster" is 92⁄6, a ratio of two noisy three-sample means. The direction is believable; the precision is not.

Second, the dramatic baselines aren't the right baselines. ReAct, Reflexion, and AutoGPT were designed for text and web tasks; re-implemented in Minecraft, ReAct and Reflexion fail to unlock any tier (0/3), and AutoGPT clears wood/stone/iron only ~10–15× slower (and never reaches diamond or solves a single zero-shot task). They lose for lack of a curriculum — not because Voyager's specific architecture is uniquely good. The comparison that actually isolates Voyager's contribution is Voyager vs. Voyager-without-skill-library, and on the early tiers that gap is small — 6 vs. 7 iterations on wood, and on the stone tier the ablation is actually faster (9 vs. 11). The skill library earns its keep later: it cuts the iron tier ~28% (21 vs. 29 iterations), is the only configuration to reach the diamond tool at all, and drives the zero-shot gains in Table 2 (Diamond Pickaxe 19 vs. 36). The purpose-built Minecraft agents from the related work — MineDojo, VPT, DreamerV3 — are surveyed but, beyond a system-level feature table, never run head-to-head on these exploration metrics (the paper deems Voyager "orthogonal" to gradient-based methods like VPT).

Third, GPT-4 has read the Minecraft wiki, and the agent grades itself. Minecraft's recipes and tech tree are exhaustively documented online and almost certainly sit in GPT-4's pretraining, so much of the "discovery" is recall of memorized game knowledge — the paper's own failure cases (the curriculum proposing a non-existent "copper sword," GPT-4 trying to burn cobblestone as an invalid fuel) show that knowledge is baked in but imperfect. And whether a task counts as done — the signal that advances the curriculum — is decided by GPT-4 critiquing GPT-4's own output: self-verification is both a core component and its own scorekeeper, with documented misses (not recognizing spider string as proof of beating a spider). The headline tier, item-count, distance, and zero-shot target-item numbers are objective game-state checks, but the per-task success that drives the loop is self-graded.

baselines

ReAct / Reflexion never unlock any tier (0/3); AutoGPT clears wood/stone/iron 3/3 but ~10–15× slower and fails diamond + every zero-shot task. The fair ablation — Voyager w/o skill library — is close on early tiers (even faster on stone, 9 vs. 11) but trails on iron (29 vs. 21) and never reaches diamond. MineDojo / VPT / DreamerV3 are surveyed, not run head-to-head.

contamination

Minecraft recipes and the tech tree are heavily documented online and very likely in GPT-4's pretraining; "open-ended discovery" leans on memorized game knowledge — the curriculum even hallucinates items (a "copper sword") that don't exist in the game.

what's missed

n = 3 with huge variance; the diamond result is a single lucky run (1/3); per-task success — which gates the curriculum — is GPT-4 self-verifying GPT-4, with known false negatives. Metrics (unique items, distance, "prompting iterations") are author-defined, not a shared benchmark.

would raise confidence

More trials (n ≥ 10) with confidence intervals; a head-to-head against MineDojo / VPT / DreamerV3 on shared MineRL tasks; a pinned, versioned GPT-4 snapshot; human-verified (not self-graded) success; and a decontamination probe of GPT-4's Minecraft priors.

// bottom line

An elegant, hugely influential blueprint for lifelong LLM agents — automatic curriculum + external code-skill memory + self-debugging — whose headline numbers are a small-n, self-graded demonstration on author-defined metrics against baselines that were never built for Minecraft. Trust the architecture; discount the multipliers.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. Voyager is the canonical "LLM agent as lifelong learner": a frozen GPT-4 writes its own curriculum, accumulates an external library of reusable code skills, and debugs itself via environment feedback + self-verification — all with zero gradient updates.
  2. Code as the action space is the load-bearing idea: programs are temporally extended and compositional, so new skills are literally built by calling old ones — ability compounds and catastrophic forgetting is sidestepped because memory lives outside the weights.
  3. The eval is a small-n (3 runs), self-verified demo on author-defined metrics; the flashy 3.3× / 15.3× gaps are mostly against text-agents re-implemented in Minecraft, while the honest ablation (no skill library) is close on the early tiers. Read Voyager for its design, not its multipliers.
